question: [File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/ISetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: setIsRootRatified] Can an unprivileged operator who has been authorized by a maker in Midnight.setIsAuthorized for unrelated position management call SetterRatifier.setIsRootRatified(maker, arbitraryRoot, true) to ratify a Merkle root the maker never intended, under the precondition that isAuthorized[maker][operator]==true in Midnight, by calling setIsRootRatified directly on SetterRatifier, violating the invariant that ratifier approval must bind correct maker intent, causing scoped impact: maker's offers are filled under terms the maker never approved? Proof idea: unit test where Alice calls Midnight.setIsAuthorized(Bob, true), Bob calls SetterRatifier.setIsRootRatified(Alice, maliciousRoot, true), Bob constructs a Merkle tree with an offer at an unfavorable tick, Bob takes the offer as taker, assert Alice's position changes without her consent.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/ISetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: setIsRootRatified] Can an authorized operator revoke a maker's active root by calling setIsRootRatified(maker, activeRoot, false) under the precondition that isAuthorized[maker][operator]==true and the maker has live offers depending on that root, by calling setIsRootRatified with newIsRootRatified=false, violating the invariant that a maker's ratified offers must remain fillable until the maker explicitly revokes them, causing scoped impact: maker's valid offers are permanently blocked (fund freeze / DoS on take)? Proof idea: unit test where maker ratifies root, operator calls setIsRootRatified(maker, root, false), assert subsequent take reverts with NotRatified.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/ISetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: setIsRootRatified] Can an authorized operator re-enable a root that the maker explicitly revoked by calling setIsRootRatified(maker, root, true) after the maker called setIsRootRatified(maker, root, false), under the precondition that isAuthorized[maker][operator]==true, by toggling isRootRatified back to true, violating the invariant that a maker's revocation of a root must be final unless the maker re-enables it, causing scoped impact: maker's revoked offers become fillable again without maker's consent? Proof idea: unit test where maker revokes root, operator re-enables it, assert take succeeds against maker's intent.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/ISetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: setIsRootRatified / Midnight.setIsAuthorized] Can a user who authorizes MidnightBundles (or any periphery contract) in Midnight inadvertently grant that contract the ability to call setIsRootRatified on their behalf, under the precondition that the user calls Midnight.setIsAuthorized(MidnightBundles, true) to use bundled flows, by MidnightBundles (or any contract with that authorization) calling SetterRatifier.setIsRootRatified(user, root, true/false), violating the invariant that authorization scope in Midnight must not silently extend to peripheral ratifier contracts, causing scoped impact: user's offer ratification state is mutated without their knowledge? Proof idea: integration test where user authorizes MidnightBundles, a malicious bundler call path invokes setIsRootRatified, assert isRootRatified state changes.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/ISetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: setIsRootRatified] Can an authorized operator ratify a root containing offers with a different market (different loanToken, collateral, maturity) than the maker intended, under the precondition that isAuthorized[maker][operator]==true and isRootRatified is not scoped to a specific market, by constructing a Merkle tree with offers for a different market and calling setIsRootRatified(maker, crossMarketRoot, true), violating the invariant that ratifier approval must bind the correct market parameters, causing scoped impact: maker's credit or debt is modified in an unintended market? Proof idea: unit test where operator ratifies a root for market B while maker only intended market A, assert take in market B succeeds.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/ISetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: setIsRootRatified / Midnight.setIsAuthorized chain] Can an authorized operator Bob (isAuthorized[Alice][Bob]==true) call Midnight.setIsAuthorized(Alice, Carol, true) to grant Carol authorization for Alice, and then Carol calls setIsRootRatified(Alice, root, true), under the precondition that Midnight authorization is coarse-grained and authorized accounts can authorize other accounts, by exploiting the authorization chain, violating the invariant that only the maker or their directly intended delegates should control root ratification, causing scoped impact: third-party Carol ratifies roots for Alice without Alice's direct consent? [File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/ISetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: isRatified / HashLib.isLeaf] Can a taker pass proof.length==256 with an arbitrary leafIndex value to bypass the LeafIndexOutOfRange check in HashLib.isLeaf, under the precondition that leafIndex>>256==0 is always true in Solidity EVM semantics for uint256, by supplying ratifierData with a 256-element proof and any leafIndex, violating the invariant that leafIndex must be bounded by the tree height, causing scoped impact: taker can attempt alternative leaf orderings against a ratified root?
